meta.description: Prefix system for the conlang. Every root word is POS-neutral — the prefix determines grammatical role, part of speech, tense, aspect, and number.

operator-plural.label: plural
operator-plural.note: Comes last in stack. Repurposed as negation on future verb 𐑛𐑮 — context disambiguates.

operator-prohibition.label: do not
operator-prohibition.note: Standalone = command not to. Also stacks onto 𐑜 for conditional prohibition.

meta.ticked_note: Ticked sounds (marked with ') are new consonants not present in English. They are difficult to produce and relatively rare, but fully part of the language. The tick indicates a distinct consonant — not a modified version of the base letter.
meta.borrow_note: Borrowed words take the generic noun prefix 𐑔 by default unless a more specific grammatical role applies.

groups.label: Noun
groups.description: A person, place, thing, or concept
groups.mnemonic: All noun prefixes use plosive stop sounds — 𐑐 𐑑 𐑒 𐑔. Mishearing one plosive for another still signals noun — the grammatical role may be uncertain but the part of speech is clear.
groups.builder_prompt: What role does this noun play in the sentence?

noun-subject.label: Subject
noun-subject.description: Who or what performs the action

noun-subject-negated.label: Subject (negated)
noun-subject-negated.example: not the dog — something else runs

noun-object.label: Object
noun-object.description: Who or what receives the action

noun-indirect.label: Indirect object
noun-indirect.description: Who or what benefits from or receives the result of the action

noun-indirect-base.label: Indirect object
noun-indirect-base.example: I gave the dog a bone

noun-indirect-negated.label: Indirect object (negated)
noun-indirect-negated.example: not for the dog — for something else

noun-generic.label: Generic noun
noun-generic.description: Standalone noun with no specific grammatical role — used for titles, list items, labels, and borrowed words
noun-generic.builder_note: This is the default prefix for borrowed words that don't have a clear grammatical role yet.

noun-generic-base.label: Generic noun
noun-generic-base.example: dictionary entry, list item, title, borrowed word

groups.label: Verb
groups.mnemonic: Tense verbs use voiced nasals and continuants — 𐑥 𐑯 𐑛. These are acoustically similar enough that mishearing one still signals verb in some tense.

verb-tense.label: Simple tense
verb-tense.description: When the action occurs — present, past, or future

verb-future-negated.label: Future (negated)
verb-future-negated.note: 𐑮 is repurposed as negation here rather than plural — verb position in the sentence disambiguates.

verb-progressive.label: Progressive
verb-progressive.description: Ongoing or continuous action. 𐑚 alone covers present progressive — the ticked forms add past and future progressive.

verb-progressive-ticked.label: Progressive (ticked — past & future)
verb-progressive-ticked.description: Ticked sounds extend the progressive to past and future, and add an emphatic present. If the tick is missed, the 𐑚 context remains readable as present progressive.

verb-progressive-past-ticked.label: Ongoing past
verb-progressive-past-ticked.fallback: 𐑚 — readable as present progressive if tick is missed

verb-progressive-emphatic-ticked.label: Ongoing present (emphatic)
verb-progressive-emphatic-ticked.example: I am eating right now — stressed
verb-progressive-emphatic-ticked.note: 𐑚 covers most present progressive cases. Use 𐑕' only when the immediacy or stress matters.
verb-progressive-emphatic-ticked.fallback: 𐑚 — use when precision isn't critical

verb-command.label: Command
verb-command.description: Imperative — telling someone to do or not do something. Phonetically distinct from all tense verbs — immediately recognizable.

groups.label: Modal
groups.mnemonic: Modals use voiced obstruents — 𐑟 𐑡 𐑜. Acoustically verb-adjacent but clearly distinct from tense verbs. Mishearing a modal still sounds verb-like.
groups.builder_note: Negation of ability (𐑟) and obligation (𐑡) uses a negative modifier word before the modal — not a stacked prefix. Only conditional (𐑜) takes a stacked negation.

modal-ability-base.label: Can / could
modal-ability-base.note: Negate with a negative modifier word before the modal, not a prefix stack.

modal-obligation-base.label: Should / must
modal-obligation-base.note: Same negation rule as ability — modifier word, not stacked prefix.

groups.label: Modifier
groups.description: Adjective or adverb. Position before the modified word determines which reading applies.
groups.mnemonic: 𐑖 and 𐑠 are fricatives — distinct from all noun and verb prefix families. 𐑠 is the dedicated negated-modifier sound, not 𐑕 stacked onto 𐑖.
groups.builder_note: Modifiers must be placed directly next to the word they modify. The sentence builder will enforce adjacency.

modifier-forms.label: Modifier forms
modifier-forms.description: Adjective or adverb — same prefix, position determines reading

modifier-base.label: Modifier
modifier-base.example: big dog / runs quickly / very happy

groups.label: Quantity / Possession
groups.description: How many of something, or to whom something belongs
groups.mnemonic: 𐑣 and 𐑞 use glottal sounds — distinct from all other prefix families. Used on numbers for quantity, on pronouns for possession.

quantity-forms.label: Quantity / possession forms
quantity-forms.description: Amount or ownership. Place before a number for quantity, before a pronoun or noun for possession.

quantity-base.label: Quantity / possession
quantity-base.example: 𐑣3 dog = 3 dogs  /  𐑣𐑰 dog = my dog

groups.label: Number
groups.description: Numeric notation — integers, decimals, fractions, and scalar expressions. Most digits carry no prefix; prefixes only mark sequence boundaries and special roles.
groups.mnemonic: Number prefixes mark where a number starts, ends, or changes register. Bare digits need no prefix — only the boundary digits get marked.

number-sequence.label: Sequence boundary markers
number-sequence.description: Mark the end of an integer, the start of a decimal, or both simultaneously

number-integer-end-decimal-follows.label: Integer end + decimal follows
number-integer-end-decimal-follows.example: 1 𐑕𐑤5 = 1.5 — integer closes and decimal begins simultaneously

number-fraction.label: Fraction marker
number-fraction.description: Divides the following noun by the specified integer denominator. Precedes the noun it applies to.

number-fraction-base.label: Fraction
number-fraction-base.example: 𐑝2 dog = half a dog  /  𐑝4 pie = a quarter of a pie
number-fraction-base.note: Use for clean integer denominators. Use decimal notation for specific decimal values.

number-scale.label: Scale operators
number-scale.description: Shift a quantity up or down by powers of ten — used for measurement, scientific notation, and large scalar expressions

number-scale-down.label: Scale down
number-scale-down.note: Not the same as fraction — 𐑝5 dog = one fifth of a dog, dog 𐑬 𐑤5 = dog × 10⁻⁵.

groups.label: Evidential
groups.description: Marks the source of the speaker's knowledge — whether they witnessed the event directly or are reporting it secondhand
groups.mnemonic: The evidential is a ticked sound — rare and deliberate. Speakers reach for it when the distinction genuinely matters, not in every sentence.
groups.builder_note: Without 𐑯' the statement is firsthand. With 𐑯' the speaker signals the information is reported, inferred, or unverified.

evidential-forms.label: Evidential forms
evidential-forms.description: Firsthand assertion vs reported / inferred speech

evidential-reportative-base.label: Reportative / evidential
evidential-reportative-base.example: 𐑯'[rain] = apparently it rained — I didn't see it  /  𐑯'[safe][bridge] = I heard the bridge is safe
evidential-reportative-base.note: Covers reported speech, inference from evidence, and hearsay. Absence of 𐑯' asserts direct knowledge.

groups.label: Ticked progressive
groups.description: Past and future progressive, plus emphatic present — ticked consonants that extend the 𐑚 progressive family to other tenses
groups.mnemonic: These are harder to produce than 𐑚 but more precise. If the tick is missed, the 𐑚 context still communicates progressive — the tense may be lost but the aspect survives.
groups.builder_note: If precision about tense isn't critical, 𐑚 covers most progressive situations. Use these when past or future progressive must be unambiguous.

ticked-progressive-forms.label: Ticked progressive forms
ticked-progressive-forms.description: Ongoing past, ongoing future, emphatic present

verb-progressive-past-ticked.label: Ongoing past
verb-progressive-past-ticked.fallback: 𐑚 — readable as present progressive if the tick is not produced

verb-progressive-emphatic-ticked.label: Ongoing present (emphatic)
verb-progressive-emphatic-ticked.example: I am eating right now — stressed immediacy
verb-progressive-emphatic-ticked.note: Use when the immediacy or stress is communicatively important. 𐑚 is the default for most present progressive.
verb-progressive-emphatic-ticked.fallback: 𐑚 — use when emphasis is not critical
